summary: Boundary semantics, WAL-before-apply ordering, and sizeBytes accounting are all correct and well tested. However, ContentStore.Append performs a fully unsynchronized read-modify-write of the content file (no per-fileID lock, unlike Catalog's striped-mutex design), causing silent lost-update data corruption under concurrent Append calls on the same fileID -- reproduced experimentally (49/50 concurrent 1-byte appends silently lost, catalog SizeBytes matched the corrupted result, no error surfaced).

requirements_conformance.evidence: sizeBytes updates correctly (engine/catalog/content.go:222 updatedRec.SizeBytes = newSize, cumulative not overwritten -- existing content is read via os.ReadFile and prepended). Threshold-crossing signal implements 'exceeds' as strict '>': thresholdCrossed := oldSize <= threshold && newSize > threshold (content.go:239). This is the correct reading of the literal acceptance-criteria word 'exceeds' (strictly greater than, not >=), and matches docs/LLD/split.md's 'Trigger: File size exceeds a tunable threshold' wording.

architecture_conformance.evidence: WAL-before-apply ordering for Append mirrors Create exactly (wal.AppendAndApply wraps writeContentFile+cat.Put, content.go:210-232) -- correct, not copy-paste-broken. However catalog.go documents striped per-fileID mutexes (~256 stripes) specifically so unrelated files don't contend and same-file ops are serialized; Append bypasses this protection for the content-file read-modify-write, which is an architectural gap relative to the module's own stated concurrency discipline (and directly relevant given split.md flags 'many goroutines appending same file simultaneously' as split's core known risk).

regression_risk.evidence: Create/Read untouched by diff; full `go test ./engine/catalog/... -race -v -count=1` passes all 1.4.1/1.4.2/1.4.3 tests plus catalog/page/WAL tests, 56s, zero failures.

edge_cases.evidence: Concurrent Append to the SAME fileID is a genuine lost-update bug: reproduced with 50 concurrent 1-byte Append() goroutines against one fileID -> final content length 1 (not 50), catalog SizeBytes silently matches the corrupted (wrong) result, no error returned to any caller. Sequential edge cases (empty-data append, single append larger than the whole threshold, append to never-created fileID) are all handled correctly (verified by inspection and TestContentAppendNotFound).

security.evidence: No new attack surface; fileID validated through cat.Get before any disk I/O; no path traversal (ContentPath derives purely from fileID uint64).

performance.evidence: O(n) full-file read + copy per append is consistent with 1.4.1/1.4.2's whole-file model; acceptable for pre-split (~8KB) file sizes per stated scope.

maintainability.evidence: Doc comments are thorough and explicit about the exactly-once crossing semantics and pre-MVCC/no-split-execution scope.

test_coverage.evidence: TestContentAppend explicitly asserts crossed matches wantCrossed at EVERY iteration (not just checking crossingCount==1), so the 'false on all appends after crossing' behavior IS actually tested, not just asserted in aggregate -- this specific ask is satisfied. Gap: no concurrent/-race test exists for Append against the same fileID, so the lost-update bug above has zero coverage and would not be caught by CI.

confidence.evidence: High confidence in the boundary-condition and WAL-ordering correctness (both directly verified); high confidence the concurrency gap is a real bug (reproduced, not theoretical).

build_and_test.go_test_race: pass, 56.192s, no failures, no regressions in 1.4.1/1.4.2 tests

recommendation: Add a per-fileID lock (or reuse Catalog's striped-mutex mechanism) around Append's read-modify-write of the content file before this path is exposed to any caller that could invoke it concurrently for the same fileID, and add a `go test -race` concurrent-Append test analogous to TestCatalogConcurrentDistinctFileIDs. Given 1.4.3 is scoped as a stub ahead of Epic 2B's real split/concurrency work, this could alternatively be explicitly documented as a single-writer-only precondition in Append's doc comment if concurrent safety is truly deferred -- but as shipped it is silently unsafe with no caller-visible warning.
